data.title: Fed yet to comply with subpoenas as part of Powell probe - report
data.description:
The Federal Reserve has yet to comply with grand jury subpoenas tied to the criminal investigation of Chair Jerome Powell, CNBC reported, citing a person familiar with the matter.

Federal prosecutors recently launched a criminal probe into Powell over the monetary authority's renovation of its Washington headquarters and whether Powell lied to Congress about the project’s scope during his congressional testimony last June.

That testimony concerned a multi-year project to renovate historic Fed office buildings—a matter Powell characterized as merely a “pretext” for the administration’s broader campaign against central bank independence.

Conducted by the office of Jeanine Pirro, the U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia, the Powell probe remains ongoing, the person told CNBC. 

There is no clear timeline for when the Fed must produce the records sought by the subpoenas.

The Federal Reserve didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

The news comes just hours before the central bank announces its latest interest-rate decision, which is widely expected to be a pause.

data.title: Financial instability risks are rising – Fmr. Atlanta Fed President Lockhart
data.description:
Dennis Lockhart, former president of the Atlanta Federal Reserve, warned that financial instability risks are rising amid a series of significant market moves that have rattled investors in recent weeks.

The veteran central banker expressed concern that these developments are creating stress for the global financial system.

In an interview with CNBC, Lockhart assessed the current market environment and its implications for monetary policy.

“It suggests that financial stability risks are rising,” he said of the recent volatility.

The dollar (DXY) has been trading at its weakest level since 2022, with the sell-off accelerating this year. Meanwhile, gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) have been hitting new record highs in what Lockhart described as a relentless march higher, and questions have emerged about long-term bonds.

Despite the mounting concerns, Lockhart said the Federal Reserve will likely take a measured approach to the turbulence.

The Fed “does not step in just with every little market upsetting or turmoil,” he explained, adding that he expects the central bank will “wait and see” as conditions develop.

Lockhart noted that the dollar’s value is primarily the U.S. Treasury’s responsibility, not the Fed’s, though coordination between the two institutions exists when necessary. While he acknowledged that the yen (FXY) situation appears to have settled down somewhat, he added that it is “troubling that the global financial system is having some of the stress.”

data.title: U.S. stocks retreat from highs as traders await Fed decision, big tech earnings amid geopolitical turmoil
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were higher on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 ((SP500)) touching on the 7,000 level in new intraday highs before paring some gains, as investors awaited the Federal Reserve's decision on interest rates and mega-cap tech company earnings.

This came as U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to attack Iran with a “massive armada” if the country does not agree to a deal over nuclear weapons.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) momentarily touched on the 7,000 mark, adding to current highs, and was last -0.1% in afternoon trade. Both the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) and the Dow (DJI) were flat.

“A massive Armada is heading to Iran… It is, ready, willing, and able to rapidly fulfill its mission, with speed and violence, if necessary… Don’t make that happen again,” President Trump said on Truth Social, earlier on Wednesday, referencing a previous military operation he called “Operation Midnight Hammer.”

Traders are also awaiting the Fed's decision this afternoon on interest rates, which are expected to remain at the current rate of 3.5%-3.75%.

“The big question isn’t what the U.S. Federal Reserve will do with rates—the market has priced the probability of a change (i.e., cut) at a de minimis 3%. Instead, the focus is on how central bankers characterize the economic outlook and policy implications,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) continued reaching new highs, surpassing $5,300 per ounce on Wednesday, while silver (XAGUSD:CUR) reached the $115 mark.

Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar (DXY) fell to its lowest level in almost four years due to geopolitical risks, intervention in the Japanese yen, and the Fed’s interest rate decision.

In addition, major tech company earnings will arrive postmarket, including Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), and Tesla (TSLA).

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields were virtually unchanged, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) 1 basis point higher at 4.26%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.59%.

“The bond market, precious metals, and currencies seem on edge…there’s a bit of déjà vu with regards to the interplay of the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y), the dollar index (DXY), and gold (XAUUSD:CUR),” said Jurrien Timmer, director of global macro at Fidelity.

“Tariffs or not, the world order is changing from a dollar-only standard to multiple spheres of influence, and the markets are taking notice. In fact, one could argue that all commodities are becoming strategic assets in this multipolar world,” he added.

data.title: Greenland Technologies prices $6.1M underwritten public offering
data.description:
• Greenland Technologies (Nasdaq: GTEC) on Wednesday said it has priced an underwritten public offering of 5,083,330 units at $1.20 per unit.
  • Each unit consists of one ordinary share and four-fifths of one warrant, with each whole warrant exercisable for one ordinary share at $1.20 per share. The warrants are immediately exercisable and will expire three years from issuance.
  • Gross proceeds from the offering are expected to be about $6.1 million before underwriting discounts and expenses.
  • The company said it plans to use the net proceeds for working capital and general corporate purposes.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about January 29, subject to customary conditions.
  • GTEC is -14.16% to $1.03.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Renasant outlines continued efficiency gains and mid-single-digit loan growth target for 2026 amid merger integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Renasant Corporation (RNST) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Kevin Chapman, President and CEO, stated that 2025 was a transformative year for Renasant, highlighting "considerable improvement in our profitability and strong balance sheet growth on the heels of the completion of the largest merger in the company's history." Chapman emphasized the company's focus on maintaining and accelerating momentum, noting that "Renasant's core profitability showed significant improvement this year, fueled by the benefits of the merger with The First, along with ongoing efforts to improve efficiency at legacy Renasant." Adjusted earnings per share for the year were $3.06, marking an 11% increase year-over-year, and the adjusted efficiency ratio improved by approximately 900 basis points to 57.46%.

  • James Mabry, Executive VP & CFO, reported that "the company's net income was $78.9 million or $0.83 per diluted share. Adjusted earnings, excluding merger charges, were $86.9 million or $0.91 per diluted share." Mabry also highlighted an adjusted return on average assets of 1.29% for the quarter, up 20 basis points from the third quarter, and an adjusted return on tangible common equity of 16.18%. Loans increased by $21.5 million on a linked-quarter basis. Mabry noted loan sales of $117 million of non-core loans and deposit growth of $48.5 million from the third quarter. "Net interest income increased $3.9 million quarter-over-quarter. Reported net interest margin increased 4 basis points to 3.89%, while adjusted margin was flat at 3.62%." Noninterest expense, excluding merger and conversion expenses, was $160.2 million, a decrease of $6.2 million from the prior quarter.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a mid-single-digit loan growth target for 2026, with Chapman stating, "We're still targeting for the year mid-single digits. And look, I think '26 can be similar to '25, where there might be some lumpiness in the quarters." Mabry addressed expense expectations, indicating that core noninterest expense is expected to decrease by $2 million to $3 million in Q1, consistent with prior guidance.

  • Chapman discussed the balance between cost savings and ongoing investments in talent, explaining that "we're still going to continue to focus on improving profitability and our expenses at Renasant. We're also going to continue to be very focused in making investments for future growth and future profitability."

  • In terms of capital deployment, Mabry said, "the most attractive one to us would be buybacks. And of course, we had some activity in Q4 and would anticipate that activity continues into '26."

Financial Results

  • Net income for the quarter was $78.9 million and adjusted earnings, excluding merger charges, were $86.9 million. Adjusted pre-provision net revenue stood at $118.3 million. The company recorded a credit loss provision on loans of $10.9 million, with net charge-offs of $9.1 million. The allowance for credit losses as a percentage of total loans declined to 1.54%.

  • Noninterest income was $51.1 million, an increase of $5.1 million compared to the prior quarter, including $2 million from the exit of certain low-income housing tax credit partnerships. Net interest income increased by $3.9 million. Noninterest expense, excluding merger and conversion expenses, decreased to $160.2 million, benefiting from $2.1 million in gains from branch consolidations.

Q&A

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about the trajectory of expenses and the impact of merger cost savings. Mabry answered that core expenses are expected to come down by $2 million to $3 million in Q1, and salaries showed the most significant impact.

  • Rose followed up on loan growth outlook. Chapman confirmed the mid-single-digit growth target for 2026 and noted that "there is the opportunity for upside as market disruption occurs."

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: Questioned the balance between investing in growth and managing expenses. Chapman explained that the company is focusing on accountability and high performance, making selective investments in both production and back office for scalability.

  • Scouten inquired about capital deployment. Mabry stated that organic growth remains the top priority, but buybacks are the most attractive capital lever in the near term. Chapman added that M&A remains on the table if the right opportunity arises.

  • David Bishop, Hovde Group: Asked about the net interest margin outlook. Mabry indicated that margin is expected to be stable in 2026, with modest growth in net interest income as the balance sheet grows.

  • Jordan Ghent, Stephens: Asked for details on the loan sale and whether more sales are expected. Mabry clarified the sale was of loans secured by cash surrender value of life insurance policies and described them as non-core; no further sales are anticipated.

  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Inquired about buyback levels and CET1 targets. Mabry stated the company aims to end 2026 with a CET1 ratio roughly unchanged from year-end 2025.

  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked if management is satisfied with current profitability metrics or seeking further improvement. Chapman responded that while goals set 18 months ago have been met, "our goal is to continue to improve from here and chase a moving target with the ultimate goal of being high performing."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for more details on cost saves, capital deployment, and growth outlook, displaying a slightly positive to neutral tone with a focus on sustainability and upside potential.

  • Management's tone was confident and optimistic during prepared remarks, emphasizing momentum and strategic flexibility. During Q&A, management remained consistent, addressing questions directly and reiterating guidance, with Chapman noting, "our execution is what will make the difference against the peer group."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained attentive to cost and growth, while management showed increased confidence backed by improved results and integration progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw management reiterate its mid-single-digit loan growth target and projected further core expense reductions, consistent with the previous quarter's guidance.

  • Efficiency improvements were realized, with the adjusted efficiency ratio improving to 57.46%, alongside higher adjusted return on tangible equity and ROA.

  • The company completed a significant non-core loan sale, which was not present in the previous quarter.

  • Analyst questions continued to focus on operating leverage, cost controls, and capital allocation, with an increased emphasis on capital deployment options such as buybacks.

  • Management's confidence in achieving and exceeding earlier merger-related performance targets was more pronounced this quarter, while analysts continued to seek clarity on recurring and sustainable improvements.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the potential for elevated loan payoffs as a wildcard impacting net loan growth on a quarterly basis, though the full-year outlook remains intact.

  • Competitive pressures, particularly on the deposit side, were highlighted, with Mabry noting, "it's very competitive on both sides. I would say probably a little more competitive incrementally on the deposit side."

  • No additional non-core loan sales are anticipated, and credit quality remains under close watch, but no significant concerns were flagged.

Final Takeaway

Renasant management highlighted a year of transformation and successful integration, achieving targeted improvements in profitability, efficiency, and growth. The company remains committed to further cost reductions and disciplined investments in talent, aiming for mid-single-digit loan growth and stable margins in 2026. Management maintains that ongoing industry disruption creates opportunities for market share gains, while capital deployment will continue to prioritize organic growth and shareholder returns through buybacks. The leadership team expressed confidence in exceeding past targets and pursuing high-performance benchmarks as the company advances into the new year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Provident projects 4–6% loan and deposit growth in 2026 while advancing core system conversion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Provident Financial Services, Inc. (PFS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Anthony Labozzetta highlighted that "The Provident team delivered another strong quarter, driven by record revenues, favorable credit metrics and expanding core profitability." The company reported net earnings of $83 million or $0.64 per share for the quarter, with an annualized return on average assets of 1.34% and an adjusted return on average tangible common equity of 17.6%. Labozzetta emphasized record pre-provision net revenue of $111 million and sustained quarterly growth following the Lakeland transaction.
  • Labozzetta reported that the commercial loan team generated total new loan production of $3.2 billion in 2025, resulting in net commercial loan growth of 5.5% for the year. The loan pipeline at year-end stood at $2.7 billion with a weighted average rate of 6.22%, and originations peaked at over $1 billion in the fourth quarter. Core deposits grew $260 million, or 6.6% annualized, compared to the prior quarter.
  • The CEO noted, “We generated record fee revenue of $28.3 million in the quarter,” with Provident Protection Plus and Beacon Trust both cited as drivers of noninterest income growth. Nonperforming assets improved to 0.32%, and share repurchase authorization was announced for up to 2 million additional shares. Preparations are underway for a core system conversion in fall 2026.
  • CFO Thomas Lyons stated, “We reported net income of $83 million or $0.64 per share for the quarter with a return on average assets of 1.34%... Revenue increased to a record for a third consecutive quarter at $226 million, driven by record net interest income of $197 million and record noninterest income of $28.3 million.” Lyons also projected continued core net interest margin (NIM) expansion of 3 to 5 basis points over the next two quarters, with reported NIM estimated in the 3.4% to 3.5% range for 2026.

Outlook

  • Lyons provided guidance for 2026, stating, “We are expecting loans and deposits to grow in the 4% to 6% range, noninterest income to average $28.5 million per quarter and are targeting a core return on average assets in the 120% to 130% range with a mid-teens return on average tangible common equity.”
  • The company expects quarterly core operating expenses of approximately $118 million to $120 million for 2026, with higher run rates in the second half due to the core system conversion and related nonrecurring charges of approximately $5 million. The effective tax rate is projected at approximately 29% for 2026.

Financial Results

  • Provident reported net earnings of $83 million or $0.64 per share, pre-provision net revenue of $111 million, and total revenue of $226 million for the fourth quarter. Net interest income reached $197 million, and noninterest income totaled $28.3 million.
  • Average earning assets grew by $307 million versus the trailing quarter, while the average yield on assets decreased 10 basis points to 5.66%. The cost of interest-bearing liabilities declined 13 basis points to 2.83%, resulting in a core net interest margin increase to 3.01%.
  • Period-end loans held for investment increased $218 million, and period-end deposits rose $182 million compared to the prior quarter. Asset quality remained strong with net charge-offs of $4.2 million and nonperforming assets at 32 basis points of total assets. The allowance coverage ratio was 95 basis points at year-end.

Q&A

  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler: Asked about the timing of tax credit benefits and capital deployment priorities. Lyons responded, “So that was in the Q4. Those are 2025 tax year benefits... Next year’s purchases, the 2026 year will be realized in 2026 as a reduction.” Labozzetta emphasized organic growth as the primary focus, with dividends and potential M&A as secondary considerations.
  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: Inquired about deposit competition, loan repricing, and CRE market trends. Labozzetta said, “Competition is heightening a little bit, but I see the competition for deposits in our market as being universal... we produced nearly $479 million of commercial deposits this year.” Lyons noted, “The total repricing over the next 4 quarters... is about $5.7 billion,” with the rate pickup adding about 4 basis points to NIM.
  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group: Explored loan yield potential, fee trends, and geographic expansion. Lyons indicated stable to slightly improving yields, while Labozzetta described ongoing talent additions and geographic penetration as expected.
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Focused on purchase accounting accretion, back book repricing, and hiring plans. Lyons estimated $60 million in purchase accounting accretion for 2026 and confirmed a hiring focus in middle market, insurance, and treasury management.
  • David Storms, Stonegate: Asked about future deposit cost reductions and tech investments. Lyons outlined further potential for lower deposit costs as rate cuts flow through, and Labozzetta detailed the upcoming core systems conversion as a major tech initiative.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, congratulating management and focusing on operational execution, competitive pressures, and growth strategies.
  • Management’s tone was confident and forward-looking in both prepared remarks and Q&A. Labozzetta and Lyons repeatedly emphasized sustained organic growth, ongoing investments, and strong credit quality, using statements like, “We are pleased with our organic growth momentum and improved profitability.”
  • Compared to the prior quarter, the tone remained constructive, with management expressing increased confidence in growth initiatives and operational leverage.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to explicit projections for 2026, including a 4% to 6% growth target for both loans and deposits, and new details on the system conversion timeline.
  • Strategic focus continues to center on organic growth, expansion in middle market and fee-based businesses, and technology upgrades.
  • Analysts’ questions this quarter were more focused on capital deployment, competitive pressures, and specifics of the upcoming system conversion, compared to last quarter’s emphasis on loan portfolio mix and operational efficiency.
  • Key metrics improved, including record revenue, pre-provision net revenue, and noninterest income, while asset quality remained strong. Management confidence in sustaining performance and executing investment initiatives increased, as evidenced by more detailed forward guidance and system conversion plans.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing competition for deposits as a challenge, noting, “everybody is in a fight for... low-cost money.”
  • The company addressed CRE market health and exposure, emphasizing that New York City rent-stabilized multifamily exposure is less than 1% of total loans and remains performing.
  • Analysts raised concerns about future deposit cost reductions, CRE portfolio acquisitions, and loan repricing risk, with management indicating mitigation through talent investments, product diversification, and disciplined underwriting.

Final Takeaway

Provident Financial Services concluded the quarter with record profitability, robust loan and deposit growth, and steady asset quality. The company is projecting 4% to 6% growth in loans and deposits in 2026, supported by new investments in talent, platforms, and a major system conversion. With continued focus on organic growth, disciplined credit, and operational efficiency, management remains confident in achieving its strategic and financial objectives for the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Corning upgrades Springboard plan to add $11B in annualized sales by 2028 as new Meta deal boosts outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Corning Incorporated (GLW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Wendell Weeks, Chairman, President & CEO, announced "Year-over-year, sales grew 14% to $4.41 billion and EPS grew 26% to $0.72. We expanded operating margin 170 basis points to 20.2%, achieving our Springboard target a full year early. And we expanded ROIC 150 basis points to 14.2%." Weeks highlighted the success of the Springboard plan, stating the company "nearly doubled free cash flow in 2025 to $1.72 billion from $880 million in 2023."
  • Weeks revealed a major long-term agreement with Meta, noting "Corning and Meta announced a multiyear up to $6 billion agreement to support Meta's apps, technologies and AI ambitions using our newest innovations in optical fiber, cable and connectivity solutions." He emphasized, "Meta will serve as the anchor customer for the expansion and upgrading our manufacturing and technology capabilities across our operations in North Carolina."
  • Weeks stated "we are upgrading our original Springboard plan to now add $11 billion in incremental annualized sales by the end of 2028, up from our original $8 billion."
  • Edward Schlesinger, Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer, said, "Year-over-year in Q4, sales grew 14% to a record $4.4 billion. EPS grew 26% to $0.72. Operating margin expanded 170 basis points to 20.2%. ROIC grew 150 basis points to 14.2%, and we delivered strong free cash flow of $732 million." Schlesinger added, "In Optical Communications, Q4 sales were $1.7 billion, up 24% year-over-year. Net income was $305 million, up 57% year-over-year, and net income margin was 18%."

Outlook

  • Weeks projected, "In quarter 1, we expect year-over-year growth to accelerate with core sales up approximately 15% to a range of $4.2 billion to $4.3 billion."
  • Schlesinger provided guidance: "We expect EPS to grow significantly faster at about 26% to a range of $0.66 to $0.70."
  • Management noted plans to increase capital expenditures to about $1.7 billion in 2026, with a focus on optical communications and customer-supported investments.
  • The upgraded Springboard plan now targets $6.5 billion in incremental annualized sales by the end of 2026, up from the prior $6 billion plan, and $5.75 billion for the high confidence plan, up from $4 billion.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter sales reached $4.4 billion, up 14% year-over-year, and EPS was $0.72, up 26% from the prior year.
  • Operating margin expanded to 20.2%. Free cash flow for the quarter was $732 million, and $1.7 billion for the full year.
  • Optical Communications led segment growth, with Q4 sales at $1.7 billion, driven by Gen AI product adoption. The enterprise business grew 61% year-over-year for the full year, with hyperscale data center growth outpacing that.
  • Display segment Q4 sales were $955 million and net income was $257 million. Specialty Materials delivered Q4 sales of $544 million, up 6% year-over-year. Automotive segment sales were $440 million, down slightly year-over-year.
  • Hemlock and Emerging Growth Businesses Q4 sales were $526 million, up 62% versus the prior year, with solar ramp-up impacting margins.

Q&A

  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA Securities: Asked if similar long-term agreements to the Meta deal are included in Springboard and about fiber market supply constraints. Weeks responded, "We have not yet included everything that those could mean because we have yet to conclude all of those agreements...we are dedicating capacity for these customers, and we're in the process of building now."
  • Mohan followed up on pricing impact. Weeks said, "Whenever we create this much value, usually some of that value creation will end up accruing to our shareholders."
  • Joshua Spector, UBS: Asked about Meta's share in enterprise sales. Weeks clarified, "Our enterprise business was about $3 billion for the year. Roughly 2/3 of that would be the hyperscalers, of which Meta was one."
  • Meta Marshall, Morgan Stanley: Asked about CapEx. Schlesinger replied, "We plan to spend about $1.7 billion in CapEx. For reference, we spent a little under $1.3 billion this year."
  • George Notter, Wolfe Research: Inquired about customer-funded CapEx for Meta. Schlesinger said, "For sure, some of the capital we plan to spend in 2026 is for the Meta deal."
  • Steven Fox, Fox Advisors: Questioned geographic production mix and margin implications. Weeks stated, "We, today are about 60% outside the U.S. and about 40% in. And we would expect something in that zone to continue."
  • Asiya Merchant, Citi: Asked about scale-up opportunity in optical. Weeks responded, "We do not have a significant revenue amount for scale-up included in this most recent Springboard upgrade. So that would be on top, depending on your opinion on timing."
  • Timothy Long, Barclays: Asked about carrier business cyclicality. Schlesinger said, "In 2025, our business was up about 15%. Majority of that growth was data center interconnect."
  • John Roberts, Mizuho: Asked about bare fiber usage. Weeks stated, "I don't actually know the answer to that question off the top of my head...let us take a moment to gather that information and we'll chat with you."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest and some skepticism about the inclusion of large customer agreements in targets and the impact of supply on growth, but tone was generally positive with a focus on details about growth drivers and risk sharing.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone throughout, especially in prepared remarks, using language such as "We feel great about our position entering 2026" and "we're seeing remarkable demand for our innovations." During the Q&A, management was more cautious and detailed, particularly regarding unclosed agreements and timing of new opportunities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained inquisitive but pressed more on the specifics of the Meta deal and capacity expansions, while management’s tone shifted from celebrating Springboard achievements to emphasizing the upgraded growth opportunity and risk-sharing with customers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Springboard plan was significantly upgraded, now targeting $11 billion in incremental annualized sales by 2028 versus $8 billion previously. The 2026 incremental sales target also increased.
  • Management’s confidence appears to have grown, bolstered by the Meta agreement and similar deals in negotiation, as opposed to last quarter’s focus on Apple’s partnership and display segment stability.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from modeling incremental margins and supply constraints to probing the durability and breadth of new customer-funded capacity agreements.
  • Key metrics such as revenue, EPS, and operating margin improved quarter-over-quarter, and free cash flow nearly doubled versus two years ago.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged the ongoing cost drag from the solar ramp, noting a temporary EPS impact of approximately $0.03 to $0.05 expected in Q1 2026.
  • The company is taking steps to mitigate risk from capacity expansions by structuring customer agreements with prepayments and long-term commitments.
  • Some uncertainty remains regarding the timing and inclusion of future scale-up optical opportunities, as these are not fully reflected in current projections.
  • Management acknowledged international market shifts and potential margin impacts, but expressed confidence in maintaining strong returns through customer partnerships and innovation.

Final Takeaway

Corning management conveyed that the company has delivered record results and upgraded its Springboard plan to target $11 billion in incremental annualized sales by 2028, supported by major new agreements like the Meta partnership. The company’s financial profile has been fundamentally transformed, with margin expansion, robust cash flow, and improved visibility into long-term growth. Executives emphasized their disciplined approach to risk-sharing and investment, setting the stage for sustained profitability and shareholder value creation in the years ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DOW Q4 earnings on deck: What to expect
data.description:
Dow (DOW) is scheduled to report its results for the fourth quarter on Thursday, before market open.

Wall Street expects the company to post a loss of about $0.51 per share on a revenue of $9.47 billion, down 8.9% year over year.

Dow’s fourth quarter was marked by a series of operational and strategic updates, including the expansion of a long-term ethylene supply agreement with MEGlobal and continued investments in product innovation across electronics, beauty, and data center cooling.

During the period, the company opened its first Cooling Science Studio in Shanghai, expanded its bio-circular product offering through ISCC PLUS certification at its Freeport, Texas facility, and highlighted new launches at industry events in Asia. The company had also declared a quarterly dividend of 35 cents per share and announced workforce and recognition-related initiatives, including an expansion of its military degree equivalency program.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system, DOW is rated Hold with a score of 3.06 out of 5, earning grades of A in terms of valuation, B in revisions, but an F in terms of growth.

Seeking Alpha analyst Louis Gerard said Dow remains under pressure from weak chemical pricing and global overcapacity, but could see outsized earnings leverage if demand rebounds, citing the company’s accelerated $1 billion cost-cutting plan and restructuring of higher-cost European assets. He added that the upcoming report could clarify the industry cycle, writing that “the coming earnings release will be crucial for investors as it will potentially help us decipher where in the cycle the chemical industry is.”

Over the last two years, Dow has beaten EPS estimates 50% of the time and revenue estimates 50% of the time.

Over the past three months, EPS estimates have seen three upward revisions and nine downward revisions, while revenue estimates have recorded one upward revision and eight downward revisions.

data.title: Here's what to expect from tobacco giants' Q4 earnings
data.description:
Altria (MO) is scheduled to announce Q4 earnings on Thursday, January 29, while its competitor Philip Morris (PM) will report earnings on February 6, both before the bell.

The consensus EPS estimate for Altria is $1.32, a 2.3% Y/Y rise, while its expected to clock in revenue of $5.03B, representing a 1.4% year-on-year decline. Meanwhile, Philip Morris is seen reporting earnings of $1.70 per share on revenue of $10.40B.

Altria continues to depend heavily on traditional cigarettes, with smoke-free products making only a limited contribution to revenue and earnings, while Philip Morris benefits from strong margins and rapid growth in its smoke-free portfolio, as per Seeking Alpha analyst Ben Holden-Crowther.

The analyst believes that the relative contribution of Altria’s other products, like oral pouches, vapour, or heated tobacco products, to revenue is likely to remain broadly the same over the coming years. Altria’s oral pouches (on!) also face significant competition from Philip Morris’ ZYNs, which is one of the two main smoke-free revenue drivers for the company, he noted.

“The recent growth for ZYN oral pouches has been particularly impressive, with its sales making it the most popular smoke-free product available in the U.S. Another particularly impressive fact that puts its success into context is that it is among the top 10 brands sold in U.S. convenience stores. This demonstrates that ZYNs are becoming a mainstream consumer product alongside other convenience store leaders like Coca-Cola, Red Bull, and Celsius,” Holden-Crowther said.

PM stocks have advanced 11.4% in the year so far, while MO has gained 10.8% compared to the S&P 500's 1.94% rise.

data.title: Wall Street cheers AT&T's Q4 report and forecast; shares rise
data.description:
U.S. telecom AT&T (NYSE:T) on Wednesday reported a strong top- and bottom-line beat for the fourth quarter and provided a full-year profit outlook that was above expectations, making investors overlook its misses for some subscriber metrics. 

Shares of the company jumped more than 6% in immediate reaction to the report; the stock is up more than 5% in midday trading. 

For the full year, AT&T guided adjusted earnings per share between $2.25 and $2.35, ahead of the $2.23 per share estimate, and adjusted EBITDA growth in the 3% to 4% range, with growth reaching 5% by 2028. 

From 2026 through 2028, the company expects service revenue growth in the low-single-digit range annually and capital investment in the $23B to $24B range annually. FCF is expected to be more than $18B in 2026, $19B+ in 2027, and $21B+ in 2028.

Beginning with the first-quarter results, AT&T plans to report under new operating segments, namely "Advanced Connectivity," "Legacy," and "Latin America."

The telecom giant also expects to buy back about $8B of common stock this year and return at least more than $45B to shareholders from 2026 to 2028. It is maintaining its current annualized common stock dividend of $1.11 per share.

In Q4, the company added 283,000 fiber subscribers, ahead of the consensus estimate of 277,028. 

However, wireless postpaid phone net adds of 421,000 were below the 439,946 estimate, and wireless postpaid net additions were 641,000, below the estimates of 663,582.

Postpaid phone-only churn in Q4 was 0.98%, up 13 basis points year-over-year, and is comparable to the consensus estimate of 0.94%. Free cash flow was $4.2B vs. $4B for the same period last year.

Analysts at J.P. Morgan called the report "solid" and said the stock remains a top pick in its U.S. Equity Analyst Focus List, supported by the company's leadership position in convergence, additional market share gains following recent investments and convergence trends, medium-term EBITDA growth acceleration, and a robust capital return program. 

Evercore ISI said AT&T wrapped up the year with "solid momentum" in Q4. KeyBanc Capital Markets thinks the report was "mixed" but 2026 and long-term guidance were "positive" and are pushing the shares higher.

"What stood out to me—and the reason we see the T stock rising pre-market—is the Mobility and Consumer Wireline segments, which saw revenue growth of 5.3% and 2.9%, respectively, during the quarter," said Danil Sereda, Investing Group Leader for Beyond the Wall Investing on Seeking Alpha. "I believe that at the current convergence rate (42% of AT&T Fiber households now also utilize AT&T for wireless services), the company seems to have met the initial plans for 2025. What drove up the stock price was also T's updated guidance." 

"I like their new segment reporting structure taking effect in 2026. Overall, I'd confirm my previous Buy on T if I had to," Sereda added.

Net income attributable to common stock for the quarter was $3.8B, or $0.53 per share, compared to $4B, or $0.56 per share, for the same period last year.

Excluding items, on a per-share basis, the Dallas, Texas-based company earned 52 cents, topping the average analyst expectation by at least 5 cents.

Revenue rose 3.6% to $33.5B and was above the consensus estimate by over $600M.
